seoTitle: How to View Your Saved Outlook Email Password (2026)
metaDescription: Recover a forgotten Outlook password using Windows Credential Manager or your browser's saved password vault, without resetting your account.
intro: Outlook masks your saved password inside its settings for privacy, so you can't just look it up in the app itself. This guide shows two legitimate ways to recover a saved credential: through Windows Credential Manager or through your browser's password manager, both of which require confirming your identity locally first.

steps.heading: Open Windows Credential Manager
steps.body: Search for Credential Manager from your Windows Start menu and launch the utility.

steps.heading: Filter for your Outlook or Office entry
steps.body: Select the Windows Credentials category and look through the list for entries related to Outlook or Office.

steps.heading: Reveal the password
steps.body: Expand the credential row and click the show or reveal option, then confirm with your device PIN or Windows Hello to unmask the text.

steps.heading: Try your browser's password manager instead
steps.body: If you signed into Outlook on the web through Chrome or Edge, open the browser's password settings, search for the Outlook or Live entry, and click the eye icon to reveal it.

steps.heading: Verify the recovered password works
steps.body: Test the password by signing back into your account to confirm it's current and correct.

faq.question: Can I view my Outlook password directly inside the Outlook app?
faq.answer: No, Outlook masks passwords in its settings for privacy, so you need to use Windows Credential Manager or your browser's password manager instead.

faq.question: Do I need my Windows PIN to reveal a saved password?
faq.answer: Yes, revealing a masked credential typically requires confirming your identity with your device PIN or Windows Hello first.

faq.question: What if the credential isn't listed in Credential Manager?
faq.answer: If nothing shows up there, check your browser's saved passwords instead, since some accounts are cached at the browser level rather than the OS level.

faq.question: Is it safe to view a saved password this way?
faq.answer: Yes, as long as you're doing it on your own trusted device, since the process requires local authentication before revealing anything.
